speaker: interviewer
text: This is July 7, 1995. We are interviewing Magda Mautner Marx. This is tape two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just let me know when you are done, David.

speaker: interviewer
text: He'll say speed. And we'll start again.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, he's actually rolling right now. Well, he's lying down--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whatever it is. When he says speed, I know we're on--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --line. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, before my husband was still in Hungary, I got again pregnant. And in 1942-- 19 of February-- I gave birth to my son, Michael. At that time, his name was [HUNGARIAN], Hungarian. And he was 6 months old when my-- when my husband was taken away. Before he was born, we hired a girl, Irene Gyigor, because I was working in the business. I couldn't stay home. And we wanted that in my last months of the pregnancy, I have some help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- after the birth of my son, I got very sick. And Irene took care of my baby. And she was-- I couldn't be such a good mother as she was. She hired a woman who nursed my son. And I-- later, I heard that she put my son on the scale to see if he had enough, and then let her to feed the-- her own, the-- the-- this woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I-- I got better. I came home, and my husband was there for a few months. And the-- the life seemed to be normal, as-- as far as could have be in this conditions. So in-- in 1942-- October-- my-- my husband was taken to Ukraine. And I told you--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he taken? Did-- did--

speaker: interviewee
text: He had to go to [NON-ENGLISH] again. He got summons. And then, well, they were there about three weeks, four weeks. And in 28th of November, they were taken to Ukraine. I have the last letter, what he wrote from there and notified me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then it come-- it was very hard on me. I was there with a little baby. We didn't know what-- what is what. And I was desperate.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sitting nights. I came home from the business, from work. I was sitting 2, 3, 4 o'clock at night, looking in the empty wall. I was not even sad. I was just empty. And one day, I called my girlfriend, whose husband was also with the same groups and had a same age little boy. I said, Olga, we cannot do that. We will get depressed. We will get sick. We have a responsibility. We have to raise all the children. We have to get out, get back to life somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, how should we do that? I said, I don't know how should we do that. Maybe let's go-- there were a beautiful hotels on the shore of the Danube. Let's go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They have every Sunday afternoon is this 5 o'clock tea. Let's go there. Let's get between people. Let's-- let's-- let's start to live because we cannot do that. We don't know what-- what wait for us. And we cannot get sick. We have to be strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one Sunday afternoon, we went there. It was terrible feeling, terrible feeling. And happened so that behind us were sitting two men. They were-- they were reading German paper. And I was so stupid. I said, yeah, you see? These goddamn Germans-- they are everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not yet in Hungary. This was in 1943. No, 1944, January. My husband was already a year missing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my friend said, don't-- don't talk so loud. Maybe they'll understand. I said, ah, don't-- you see that they read German paper. But then, we stood up. And I saw that these two men fold the paper and standing up also.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, listen. Something is-- something is not right. Now, let's see. We go to the ladies room. If these two men doesn't go away, then when we come out, we go, and we say goodbye to each other. You say one way. I go one way. You go the other way. And if they follow us, now we promise to each other if one of us-- since I was who said the stupid thing, promise me that you will take care of my son if something happens to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and we-- we left. And then I heard-- it was already dark outside. And I heard that, yeah, I am followed. So and this man caught up with me. And he said, don't be afraid. You were very silly to say this, what you said. But I am a Jew. So don't be afraid of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it is-- I-- I will take you home. It's already dark, and don't go alone. So he took me home. And he-- his name was Nicolas Gelb. He was a dentist. And he was with false papers in Hungary. He just escaped from-- from the Germans. And he-- the other man followed my friend. And somehow, then we met a few times. So we started a little better life-- little normal life to live. Very short time because this was '44-- January or February. In March, the German came into Hungary already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these men-- Nicolas-- he was one who saved my life, also, because he said, now you listen to me, and remember every word that I will tell to you. Don't believe one word what the Judenrat, these-- I-- I don't know how call it in-- in English-- Council.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish council.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the Jewish council will say because they have to cooperate with the Germans. This not true that these-- they take you-- that all the people to unite with the families and to work. They are gas chambers. And the people who they take will be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you listen. Do you trust Irene? I said, with my whole life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Send her home, and let her bring her papers for you. And you find paper-- attrition paper for your son. And be very careful. Watch very carefully what happens. And you have to know which is the point or which is the day when you have to disappear. Go and be somebody else. Go hide-- be hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I couldn't send Irene home because I still was working, and I needed her. But I had a neighbor, Mrs. Horvat. And she had a-- a baby as the-- as the same age as my son was. Even they had-- was on [INAUDIBLE] also. So I decided that [? lose ?] what I not [? write ?] doesn't appeal to me. I [? have had ?] to follow. I learned what-- what Nicolas said. But in the meantime, what happened? The German came in. And he-- this Nicolas-- had to go-- was-- was an office where everybody alien had to go and every month to report that they are there, and they are not depending on the-- on the support of the country. This-- and he had to go there. This was a Thursday. And never called me, never-- I didn't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a few days later, I got a postcard thrown out from somewhere-- from a-- from a wagon. But he wrote, I am on my way. I don't know where. Maybe we will see each other someday. But in the meantime, remember what I told you. And follow what I told you. I am worrying about you and [HUNGARIAN], my son. He was-- he was already in the-- they deported, and he was worrying about us. I never had heard about him. But this-- did what he said. And the first thing what the Germans did when they came to Hungary in March 19, 1944, was-- the first rule-- everybody had to take the radios. The Jewish people shouldn't have a radio. So I threw the radio. I don't know where was it. I had never anybody came to check if I gave the radio. So I thought, oh, I did this. So that was the last thing that I did. I didn't take-- [? work ?] the rules. Give all the money, the jewelry, everything-- I didn't. In the meantime, we-- oh, at the building that I used to live was designated as a Jewish house. What meant that they put a big yellow star on the-- on the entrance. And we had to wear the yellow star, of course. And we had-- the Jewish people had to move in. So I had a small apartment-- only one living room, and a hall, and a little room for the bed. So I asked a friend whose husband also was taken to Ukraine to move in with me because I didn't want that they put in a whole family in my small apartment. So-- and in the meantime, with Irene-- yeah, in the meantime, Irene [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] couldn't stay with-- with us anymore because Jewish people couldn't have a Christian or gentile people in the household. So she left. And she never took a steady job. She went-- she took a-- occasional jobs to be always around us if it is something happens to us. And she came sometimes to-- to visit. I told him, Irene, don't come, because we don't know. It can happen that they will take us away. If you are here, they will take you, too. And she said, if they take you, I go where you go. It's-- it reminded me to Ruth's story from the Bible. And she did. She-- she really was that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the meantime, I took a Jewish girl because I still-- with the yellow star, I went to my business. And this went on to-- to-- in the-- in the end of August or the mid-August, started to be a little milder, not-- not so strict against the Jews. By-- by the meantime, they took away all the Jews from the country. Were no Jews anymore in the country but in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- I showed to you that one was a Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. And they permitted that we go out at the evening to go to the synagogue. Otherwise, we were permitted only from 11:00 to 5 o'clock to be out of the house. And we could go shopping only between 12:00 and 1:00. And so I was not home that time. So I-- [LAUGHS] I sent my little son before that, who was 2 years old and had not to get the yellow star. He went shopping. [LAUGHS] And he was so cute. Once, a grocer was at-- at-- at-- not a grocery store. Such a fruit store. And I told him, buy me two, three tomatoes and two green peppers. And I saw that he goes in and look the-- the tomatoes. And he bought the. He-- he told to the-- the man know me, you know, so because I was a customer, that I will pay for the.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I say, what-- what did you look? I looked at all should be red. [LAUGHS] 2 years old. So then-- what was very important. Yeah. Came a day that I was very sick. I got a-- some kind of a-- an infection-- sinus infection. And I had very high fever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And comes up one of my workers. And he said, we-- we closed the store because came out the order that the Jewish-- every Jewish store or workshop has to be closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I jumped out from my bed. Sick or not sick, I told, go back and open the business. Open the door. As long as somebody will not come and will say, this store has to be closed, I will not close.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I spread the rumor that I worked for the army. And I have a special permission. I didn't have. But people didn't think that somebody has such a chutzpah that goes to-- [LAUGHS] that will start to the business without the special permission. But then I decided that I will hire an Aryan manager. I had about-- at that time, I had about eight workers who worked for me. So I gave an advertisement in the paper. And came a very bad-looking man.

speaker: interviewer
text: I ask, are you Aryan? He showed the papers. Was OK. Do you know something about this profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I had a uncle in a town. And I was working there. He didn't have an idea, but this was not important. The important was that I had an Aryan person in the business who manage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and then came October 15, when they-- in the meantime, a-- Horthy, the governor wanted to get out of the Germans. But was too late. Was afraid that if he goes and parted-- goes away from the Germans, then the Russian will come to Hungary. And there will be communism. So he-- he denied it, and he-- then he couldn't even do it because the German captured him later. And so it came the October 15, when Szalasi, the main-- how did you call them? The Arrow-- Arrow Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arrow Cross leader took over the-- the government. And just on that days, Irene was not there because I sent her home. It looked a little better, the situation. I sent her home to bring her papers. In the meantime, I talked to this Mrs. Horvat and asked where her son was born. And I know that they were at the-- at the same age. And the-- I went to the, [LAUGHS] yeah, to the city hall. And I said, I am Mrs. Horvat. And I want to have a copy of my son's birthday certificate. So they-- they gave it to me. And on October 16-- October 15-- this was the first thing what is Szalasi said in a-- in a radio speech, that it's not anymore no Swiss [NON-ENGLISH] pass. No Swedish, no nothing. It's only Jews, and every Jews has to be killed. And just now, Irene was not there. So I was desperate, really. The next morning, in October 16, I look out of the window. And the whole house is surrounded with German soldiers, up with the weapons to the window. And they yelled, go away from the window in German. But I understand German, so I know what-- what they say. And so I-- I saw, now they will take us and-- and killing on the-- in the backyard. But they left. So later, we heard that somebody shot, maybe from this building. This-- this building-- it was very interesting why they made out of it a Jewish building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was next to the Hungarian parliament on the shore of the Danube, four buildings-- such a block. In this building, facing the parliament, lived the newspaper owner-- the biggest Nazi, [NON-ENGLISH]. Then, on the other building, what faced the Danube, they were-- they put up a German office of full with German soldiers. They always loved it. They looked over to my building so to-- to my star.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were on the street. And then the fourth building was a regular apartment house. So this was designated a small building. So it was not the real reason why to make for this a building where a lot of people can move in because they said that the English whoever comes to bomb Budapest, they will know that in this building are Jewish, and we will save the Germans [LAUGHS] and-- and [NON-ENGLISH] there. But really, they didn't bomb this building. [LAUGHS] I don't know why. But it happened. I think they didn't want to ruin the parliament.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the 16th, I didn't know what-- what to do. I wanted to save only the child. I didn't care about myself, only the child. But I couldn't find a way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that, I tried-- I took a-- there were small balconies on the buildings. And in the next hall, where this Nazi lived, it was-- there was wrought iron gates. And I tried to put over a ironing board. Maybe I can somehow escape on that some night. But this didn't work out. And on the 16th of October, at the evening, like-- like a miracle, Irene appeared. She-- well, in the-- in the town where-- where she lived, already the Russian came in. And she was escaping through forests and ditches and I don't know. They were bombing and everything above her. But she had that feeling, she has to come. She has to help us. And she arrived on the 16th. She really, really risk her life a few times for us. And so I went down to the super. And I told, let-- let Irene out with the child. No way, no way, no way. I promised him money and more money. No, he cannot do that. But his wife was always envy of the beautiful carriage what I had for my son and the clothing. So I go to give them to her. And I told Mrs. [? Braun, ?] convince your husband to let out only. You are a mother. You understand. I wanted Irene takes [HUNGARIAN]. I will give you a lot of money. And you can go up to my apartment and to take whatever you want to-- all the clothes of [HUNGARIAN], the carriage, everything what you want to. And she convinced him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I had a small clutch pocketbook. I had money in it. And-- and I had Irene's paper, too, and money to give to the-- to this man. So he opened the door. It was pitch dark outside because was blackout. And when he opened the-- the entrance gate, I jumped after Irene went out with the-- with the child. And I jumped out, without a coat, without anything. So we were out. And then I thought, now where do we go? And we cannot be together because I have different paper than the child. And when he would say once mommy or something, then we are lost both. Said, no, no, I will take [HUNGARIAN]. And so they-- I thought, do you know somebody who I can trust and to go there? And she said, on-- in Buda, on a place-- they call it the Rose Hill. It was all villas-- there a beautiful place-- there is a woman who knows her. And she knows the address. And I should go there and ask that she-- ask her to let me in. So I-- it-- I cannot tell you the feeling. First of all, I had to take-- I saw in the night to disappear Irene with my child. I didn't know where they go. But I know that she is in good hand. This-- I didn't worry about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had to take the streetcar. I didn't have the star on me. I felt like-- like a murderer or something, that everybody looks at me. Nobody looked at me. But I had such a terrible feeling, like a criminal, because I am out without the star. And then in an hour that I not suppose. And on the streetcar was sitting a woman. There were more people. And nobody paid attention. But was sitting a woman, and in the window-- you know, the streetcar window-- I had the feeling that she is watching me. So I said, I don't get off. But one-- I had to go to the end stop. And this woman left the train at stop before. So she didn't-- this was only my feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to this woman. It was a villa with a gate and a garden. So I rung the bell, and she came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told, Irene sent me. And I want to stay one night here. No, she cannot do that. She cannot do that. Luckily, I had some money with me because I had to think ahead, you know, that maybe I will need money. So I-- she-- she-- I said, listen, I give you 300 [HUNGARIAN]-- this was a lot of money-- for one night. So she let me in. But this night was the biggest nightmare. So we went to bed. And she had one room. I was in one bed. She was in the other bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somebody rung the bell. It was about 12:00 at night. Said, everybody's at home. Who can this be?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, go and see. And she goes out and came back. Three this Arrow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cross. They were teenagers, or I don't know, but with a weapon. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
